messages.content: checked attribute not work in html radio button in angular when using ngModel

The checked attribute in HTML radio buttons does not work in Angular when using ngModel to bind a value to the radio button. This is because Angular uses its own way of binding data to elements, and the checked attribute is not part of that data binding mechanism.

To set the default selected radio button using ngModel, you need to bind the value of the selected radio button to a variable in your component. Then, you can use that variable to set the default selected radio button in your template. Here's an example:

In your component:

```
selectedOption = 'option1';
```

In your template:

```
<label>
  <input type="radio" name="options" value="option1" [(ngModel)]="selectedOption">
  Option 1
</label>

<label>
  <input type="radio" name="options" value="option2" [(ngModel)]="selectedOption">
  Option 2
</label>

<label>
  <input type="radio" name="options" value="option3" [(ngModel)]="selectedOption">
  Option 3
</label>
```

In this example, the selectedOption variable is used to bind the selected value of the radio button group. Since it is initialized to 'option1' in the component, the radio button with a value of 'option1' will be selected by default.
How to change the position of the div according the place of the selected radio button in angular

To change the position of a div based on the selected radio button, you can use Angular's ngClass directive along with CSS classes. Here's how you can do it: In your component, define a variable to keep track of the selected option: ``` selectedOption: string; ``` In your template, use ngClass to add or remove CSS classes based on the selected option: ```
``` In this example, we have two radio buttons with values of 'option1' and 'option2'. We use ngModel to bind the selected value to the selectedOption variable in the component. We then use ngClass directive with an object containing CSS classes to add or remove based on the selectedOption value. If 'option1' is selected, the 'left' class will be added to the div, and if 'option2' is selected, the 'right' class will be added. In your CSS file, you can define the 'left' and 'right' classes to position the div as you desire: ``` .left { position: absolute; left: 0; } .right { position: absolute; right: 0; } ``` This will position the div on the left or right side of the container, depending on the selected radio button. You can adjust the CSS classes and properties as needed to achieve the desired positioning.
i have the radio button in vertical

If you have the radio buttons arranged vertically, you can still use the same technique to change the position of a div based on the selected radio button. Here's an example: In your component, define a variable to keep track of the selected option: ``` selectedOption: string; ``` In your template, use ngClass to add or remove CSS classes based on the selected option: ```
``` In this example, we have two radio buttons with values of 'option1' and 'option2'. We use ngModel to bind the selected value to the selectedOption variable in the component. We then use ngClass directive with an object containing CSS classes to add or remove based on the selectedOption value. If 'option1' is selected, the 'top' class will be added to the div, and if 'option2' is selected, the 'bottom' class will be added. In your CSS file, you can define the 'top' and 'bottom' classes to position the div as you desire: ``` .top { position: absolute; top: 0; } .bottom { position: absolute; bottom: 0; } ``` This will position the div at the top or bottom of the container, depending on the selected radio button. Again, you can adjust the CSS classes and properties as needed to achieve the desired positioning.
